ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FJP covers composite salad dishes; no specific code exists for burrata and prosciutto salad. Closest_parent match used.

serving.natural_language_portion: One typical restaurant serving of Burrata and Aged Prosciutto Salad is approximately 200 g, comprising a portion of burrata cheese, several slices of aged prosciutto, a handful of arugula, cherry tomatoes, sliced peach, and balsamic vinaigrette dressing.

nutrient_source.title: Burrata & Aged Prosciutto Salad — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Burrata & Aged Prosciutto Salad (cr_id G00010964). Saturated fat estimated from component analysis (burrata ~8 g/100 g sat fat, prosciutto ~4 g/100 g sat fat at typical dish proportions) as HPB does not report saturated fat breakdown separately; documented in notes. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, and several micronutrients not reported by HPB and set to -1.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; analogous estimate based on mixed salad with cheese and cured meat components
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Burrata and Aged Prosciutto Salad. GI estimated at approximately 32 (low) based on the dominant low-GI components: burrata cheese (GI ~27, analogous to fresh mozzarella/soft cheese), arugula and cherry tomatoes (non-starchy vegetables, GI <15), peach (GI ~28–35), and balsamic vinaigrette (GI ~5). Aged prosciutto contributes negligible carbohydrate. Weighted average of carbohydrate-contributing components yields an estimated dish GI of ~32. Glycemic load per 200 g serving = (32 × 5.9 g carbohydrate × 2) / 100 = 3.8 g. This is an analogous estimate; direct measurement is recommended for clinical use.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 810 mg/100 g — well above the 600 mg/100 g high-concern threshold, driven by aged prosciutto and burrata
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit this dish; a 200 g serving delivers approximately 1620 mg sodium, representing over 70% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg, and the very low potassium content (64 mg/100 g) provides no meaningful counter-regulatory offset.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate (5.9 g/100 g) and low GI (~32) are favourable, but very high total fat (44.54 g/100 g) raises insulin resistance concern with regular consumption
type2_diabetes.guidance: The low carbohydrate content and low glycaemic index result in minimal acute glycaemic impact, making this dish acceptable for occasional consumption by people with type 2 diabetes; however, the very high fat content (44.54 g/100 g, predominantly saturated) may impair insulin sensitivity over time and portion size should be monitored carefully.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very high total fat (44.54 g/100 g) with estimated saturated fat ~14 g/100 g, high sodium (810 mg/100 g), and the presence of processed cured meat (aged prosciutto)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: This dish is not recommended for individuals with or at risk of cardiovascular disease due to its very high saturated fat content (~14 g/100 g, far exceeding the 5 g/100 g threshold), high sodium load (810 mg/100 g), and the inclusion of aged prosciutto — a processed cured meat associated with increased cardiovascular risk; if consumed, portion size should be strictly limited and frequency minimised.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Estimated saturated fat ~14 g/100 g from burrata and prosciutto far exceeds the 5 g/100 g RA threshold; prosciutto is a processed cured meat with pro-inflammatory properties
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this dish in its standard form as the high saturated fat from burrata and aged prosciutto, combined with the pro-inflammatory profile of cured meat (sodium nitrates, arachidonic acid), may exacerbate joint inflammation; reducing or omitting prosciutto and using less burrata would substantially lower the inflammatory load.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Burrata and Aged Prosciutto Salad is a Western-style composed salad originating from Italian culinary tradition and widely served in contemporary restaurant settings in Singapore and internationally. The dish combines fresh burrata cheese — a soft, creamy Italian cheese made from mozzarella and cream — with thinly sliced aged prosciutto crudo, peppery arugula leaves, sweet cherry tomatoes, sliced fresh peach, and a balsamic vinaigrette dressing. The flavour profile is a balance of rich, milky creaminess from the burrata, savoury and slightly salty notes from the prosciutto, bitterness from the arugula, sweetness from the peach and tomatoes, and acidity from the balsamic dressing. The dish is served cold and requires no cooking — it is assembled fresh. Nutritionally, it is very high in fat (44.54 g/100 g), predominantly from the burrata and prosciutto, with estimated saturated fat of approximately 14 g/100 g. Carbohydrate content is low at 5.9 g/100 g with a low glycaemic index (~32), making it minimally impactful on blood glucose. However, sodium is very high at 810 mg/100 g due to the cured prosciutto, making it unsuitable for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is not suitable for vegans or vegetarians and contains dairy allergens. It is typically served as a starter or light main course at lunch or dinner.
common_user_queries: How many calories in burrata and prosciutto salad?
Is burrata salad good for diabetics?
Can I eat prosciutto salad if I have high blood pressure?
Is burrata and prosciutto salad high in sodium?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id G00010964, confidence 0.95), which is the primary approved source for Singapore food records. Saturated fat estimated from component analysis and documented. GI is an analogous estimate from approved University of Sydney GI Database methodology, clearly documented.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (14.0 g/100 g) is an estimate derived from component analysis: burrata cheese contributes approximately 8–10 g/100 g saturated fat at typical dish proportions, and aged prosciutto contributes approximately 3–5 g/100 g; the HPB source does not report saturated fat separately. This value should be confirmed against a direct laboratory analysis or updated HPB entry when available. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, fat-soluble vitamins, B-vitamin profile, and moisture data for this composite dish are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database (G00010964) as of 2026-04-16, and no equivalent composite entry was found in USDA FoodData Central. 3. dietary_fibre_g is reported as 0.0 by the HPB source; this is confirmed zero per the HPB database entry, though the presence of arugula, cherry tomatoes, and peach would be expected to contribute some fibre — this value may reflect the specific preparation and portion ratios tested. 4. GI of approximately 32 is an analogous estimate based on the carbohydrate-contributing components (peach GI ~28–35, balsamic vinaigrette GI ~5, cherry tomatoes GI <15); direct GI measurement for this dish is not available in the University of Sydney GI Database. 5. This dish is not suitable for halal diets as prosciutto is pork-derived. 6. The dish is classified as Western cuisine; no mandatory local-language names apply under the multilingual rules (Singaporean, Japanese, Korean, Indonesian, Thai, or Vietnamese cuisine rules do not apply to Western dishes).
